name: Crypto-Mining Attack Disguised as Memory Leak
description: A malicious crypto-miner has been deployed on the payment service, consuming CPU and causing cascading failures. Restarting the service only temporarily fixes it as the malware auto-restarts.

alerts.message: payment-service timing out on 85% of requests

logs: [2026-03-25T14:00:00Z] INFO   Payment processing started for order #94521
[2026-03-25T14:00:02Z] WARN   CPU usage sustained above 95% for 15 minutes
[2026-03-25T14:00:05Z] WARN   Response time degraded: 120ms -> 12,000ms
[2026-03-25T14:00:08Z] INFO   Active payment transactions: 3 (normal load)
[2026-03-25T14:00:10Z] WARN   Memory usage climbing: 3.64GB / 4.0GB
[2026-03-25T14:00:12Z] INFO   Child process PID 9821 started at 2026-03-25T13:42:00Z
[2026-03-25T14:00:15Z] INFO   Connection pool status: 45 active, 155 idle
[2026-03-25T14:00:18Z] WARN   GC pressure increasing due to memory constraint
[2026-03-25T14:00:01Z] WARN   Payment page load time: 12,500ms
[2026-03-25T14:00:05Z] ERROR  User checkout timeout for session #88421
[2026-03-25T14:00:08Z] WARN   Upstream payment-service latency critical
[2026-03-25T14:00:02Z] ERROR  Upstream timeout: payment-service (30s)
[2026-03-25T14:00:05Z] WARN   Circuit breaker HALF-OPEN for payment-service
[2026-03-25T14:00:08Z] INFO   user-service and other endpoints healthy
[2026-03-25T14:00:00Z] INFO   Normal operation. 45 active connections.
[2026-03-25T14:00:05Z] INFO   Query latency normal: avg 12ms

notes: Process name is disguised as payment-worker-2 (looks like a legit worker). External connections show raw IPs with no labels. Agent must notice: 1) payment-worker-2 using 93% CPU but only 3 transactions active, 2) two outbound connections to unknown external IPs on unusual ports (4444, 3333), 3) PID 9821 started at an odd time and is not part of the normal process tree.

name: Cascading Failure from Corrupted Config Push
description: A bad configuration push to the service mesh sidecar has caused cascading TLS failures across the cluster. Services are failing in sequence as they try to communicate.

alerts.message: TLS handshake failures to database-primary: certificate mismatch
TLS handshake failures to cache-redis: certificate mismatch
Error rate 92% - all downstream services unreachable
All API calls failing. Site completely down.
Config push v4.1.0 completed 12 minutes ago

logs: [2026-03-25T14:48:00Z] INFO   Config push v4.1.0 initiated by deploy-bot
[2026-03-25T14:48:05Z] INFO   Distributing new sidecar TLS certificates to all services
[2026-03-25T14:48:10Z] INFO   Push completed to 6/6 services
[2026-03-25T14:48:12Z] INFO   Config version v4.1.0 active across cluster
network: ESTABLISHED connections to all service sidecars

logs: [2026-03-25T14:48:20Z] ERROR  TLS handshake failed to user-service: certificate_unknown
[2026-03-25T14:48:22Z] ERROR  TLS handshake failed to payment-service: certificate_unknown
[2026-03-25T14:48:25Z] ERROR  All downstream services unreachable via mTLS
[2026-03-25T14:48:28Z] WARN   Sidecar proxy received new certificates at 14:48:10
[2026-03-25T14:48:30Z] ERROR  Error rate: 92%. Zero successful downstream calls.
[2026-03-25T14:48:18Z] ERROR  Sidecar certificate rotated
[2026-03-25T14:48:20Z] ERROR  TLS handshake to database-primary failed: ERR_CERT_AUTHORITY_INVALID
[2026-03-25T14:48:22Z] ERROR  Cannot reach cache-redis: mTLS certificate rejected
[2026-03-25T14:48:25Z] ERROR  All dependent services unreachable
[2026-03-25T14:48:19Z] ERROR  Sidecar certificate rotated
[2026-03-25T14:48:21Z] ERROR  TLS handshake to database-primary failed
[2026-03-25T14:48:24Z] ERROR  All database operations failing

notes: The config-server itself appears healthy (status=healthy, green metrics). No rollback hint in logs. The trap is that all alerts point to downstream services. Agent must trace the common pattern (all TLS failures started at the same time) and correlate with the config-server deployment log to identify the root cause. Simply restarting individual services won't help because they'll load the same bad config.

name: DDoS Attack vs Legitimate Traffic Spike
description: The frontend is receiving massive traffic. The agent must determine if this is a legitimate viral event or a DDoS attack, and apply the correct mitigation.

alerts.message: Max connections reached. Dropping new requests.

logs: [2026-03-25T16:00:00Z] WARN   Traffic spike detected: 50,000 req/sec (baseline: 2,000/sec)
[2026-03-25T16:00:02Z] INFO   Unique source IPs in last 60s: 12,847
[2026-03-25T16:00:06Z] INFO   Request breakdown: /api/search 47,120 req/sec, /api/users 1,450 req/sec, / 1,430 req/sec
[2026-03-25T16:00:08Z] INFO   Average request payload size: 43 bytes
[2026-03-25T16:00:10Z] INFO   User-Agent distribution: 78% identical UA string 'Mozilla/5.0 (compatible)'
[2026-03-25T16:00:12Z] WARN   Geographic distribution: 89% of traffic from 3 ASNs
[2026-03-25T16:00:15Z] ERROR  Connection pool exhausted. Dropping connections.
[2026-03-25T16:00:03Z] ERROR  Max connections reached: 500/500
[2026-03-25T16:00:05Z] ERROR  Dropping incoming requests due to capacity
[2026-03-25T16:00:08Z] WARN   Both legitimate and excess traffic being dropped
[2026-03-25T16:00:05Z] WARN   Read query surge: 8,000 queries/sec (normal: 200/sec)
[2026-03-25T16:00:08Z] WARN   CPU at 88% from query volume increase
[2026-03-25T16:00:10Z] INFO   Query pattern: repeated SELECT on 'search' table with varying parameters

notes: Clues spread across logs and network: 1) /api/search receives 94% of traffic while other endpoints are normal, 2) 43-byte uniform request size, 3) 78% identical user-agent, 4) 89% traffic from 3 ASNs, 5) 3 subnets dominate connections. Agent must read logs AND check network to piece together the DDoS pattern. No single log line spells it out.
